title: Shared KV: tying keys and values in multi-head attention
text:
Standard multi-head attention projects the input into separate queries, keys, and values via \(W_Q\), \(W_K\), \(W_V\). Shared KV eliminates the separate key projection: a single matrix \(W_{KV}\) produces a representation that is used as both the value and (after RoPE) the key, halving the KV cache at inference. The modification was tested at 270M and 1B parameter scales on C4 on a v4-32 TPU pod slice.
At 270M with matched learning rate (\(3 \times 10^{-4}\)), shared KV (EMA loss 2.88) is 0.025 nats behind the standard baseline (2.85). At 1B, shared KV (2.51) is 0.018 nats behind the baseline (2.49). Both gaps are small relative to the 50% KV cache reduction.

title: Shared KV: tying keys and values in multi-head attention
text:
Limitations

All baselines use MHA (n_kv=16, n_q_per_kv=1). No comparison against GQA or MQA, which also reduce KV cache.
Only one shared KV run exists at each scale.
No eval loss, perplexity, or downstream task metrics were recorded. All comparisons use training loss only.
No inference latency or KV cache memory measurements were taken.

title: Shared KV: tying keys and values in multi-head attention
text:
Next experiments
Shared KV halves KV cache by tying K and V. GQA with n_kv=8 also halves KV cache relative to MHA with n_kv=16. The two strategies achieve the same 2x KV compression through different mechanisms and can be combined. The relevant comparisons at 270M and 1B:

title: PyTree-zarr checkpoint format
text:
For seqax we write checkpoints of JAX PyTrees, in a simple format documented here.


The zarr of a PyTree is a a zarr Group with the following elements: * for each path, array in the flattened PyTree, the zarr Group contains array as a child array, with path equal to jax.tree_util.keystr(path) * additionally there is a zarr attribute by name write_completed and value True.
The zarr of a PyTree may be written to disk with any compression and chunk size settings.



We use zarr to support parallel writers from different hosts in a fully-sharded training setup. (Parallel writers in this scenario must choose a chunk size that divides the data size per host, so as to avoid zarr race conditions during writing.) Readers of the checkpoint format do not need to be aware that it was written in parallel, as this is hidden by the zarr abstraction.
We use the write_completed attribute to allow parallel writers to support a “two phase commit” protocol: all writers write their data chunks, then wait for a global barrier, then the “leader” writer sets the write_completed attribute. This protects readers from reading partially-written checkpoints.

title: Position embeddings: CoPE vs ALiBi vs RoPE
text: Three position embedding methods were compared across multiple datasets and model scales: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE), Contextual Position Encoding (CoPE), and Attention with Linear Biases (ALiBi). RoPE is the default in seqax. CoPE and ALiBi were implemented on feature branches and tested against RoPE baselines. RoPE consistently matches or outperforms both alternatives at the 31M parameter scale tested. All comparisons use EMA-smoothed final training loss.

title: Position embeddings: CoPE vs ALiBi vs RoPE
text:
Limitations

31M parameters only. Results may differ at larger scales where long-range dependencies matter more.
Context lengths are 1024 tokens. ALiBi and CoPE target length generalization, which is not tested here.
Cross-dataset comparisons are approximate. Only within-dataset comparisons at the same LR and step count are controlled.
No inference latency, memory, or length-generalization measurements were taken.

title: Review: Power Scheduler (Shen et al., 2024)
text:
Power Scheduler: A Batch Size and Token Number Agnostic Learning Rate Scheduler proposes a learning rate schedule derived from an empirical power-law relationship between the optimal learning rate, batch size, and number of training tokens. The schedule continuously decays the learning rate as \(\eta(n) = \beta \cdot a \cdot n^b\) (where \(n\) is tokens seen so far), eliminating the need to specify total training steps in advance — unlike cosine decay or the stable phase of WSD. Combined with Maximum Update Parameterization (muP), the authors claim a single set of hyperparameters transfers across token counts, batch sizes, model sizes, and architectures (dense and MoE).
We ran a 95-run HPO sweep at 37M scale to test whether the power-law schedule improves over cosine decay in our codebase. It does not — the best power-law configurations match but do not beat the cosine baseline at 2900 steps. The rest of this document reviews the paper’s claims, then presents our replication results.

title: Review: Power Scheduler (Shen et al., 2024)
text:
Paper summary

The power-law relationship
The core empirical finding comes from exhaustive grid searches over three proxy model sizes (12M, 36M, 121M parameters) trained on RedPajama. For each combination of training tokens \(T \in \{2\text{B}, \ldots, 256\text{B}\}\), batch size \(\beta \in \{16, \ldots, 512\}\), and learning rate \(\eta \in \{0.0002, \ldots, 0.0256\}\), they find the loss-minimizing learning rate. The result: \(\eta_\text{opt} = \beta \cdot a \cdot T^b\), with fitted coefficients \(a \approx 4.6\), \(b \approx -0.51\). The exponent being close to \(-0.5\) (inverse square root of tokens) is suggestive but unexplored by the authors.


The schedule
Three phases:

Warmup. Linear ramp to \(\eta_\text{power}(N_\text{warmup})\).
Power phase. \(\eta(n) = \min(\eta_\text{max},\; \beta \cdot a \cdot n^b)\) — the learning rate continuously decays as a function of tokens seen, unlike WSD which holds constant then decays.
Decay phase. Exponential decay to 0 over the final ~10% of training.

The authors use \(a = 4\), \(b = -0.51\), \(\eta_\text{max} = 0.02\) for all experiments. The reduction from the empirically fitted \(a = 4.6\) to the deployed \(a = 4\) is not explained.
Our implementation follows Section 4 directly. The power phase computes \(\eta(n) = \min\!\bigl(\eta_\text{max},\; \beta \cdot a \cdot (\underbrace{n \cdot \beta \cdot L}_{\text{tokens seen}})^{b}\bigr)\) where \(\beta\) is batch size and \(L\) is sequence length. The cooldown uses exponential half-life decay: \(\eta_\text{decay}(n) = 0.5^{(n - S)/T} \cdot \eta_\text{power}(S)\), where \(S\) is the step where cooldown begins and \(T\) is cooldown length (10% of total steps). This means the final learning rate is \(0.5 \times \eta_\text{power}(S)\), not zero — a detail the paper leaves unspecified.


Experimental claims
At 1B scale (1T tokens, controlled comparison), the Power Scheduler achieves 58.2% average zero-shot accuracy vs. 57.7% for cosine and 57.6% for WSD — a 0.5-point improvement. At 3B scale, PowerLM-3B (66.8% average multiple-choice) is competitive with Gemma2-2B (66.7%) but notably weaker on MMLU (49.2% vs. 53.0%).

title: Review: Power Scheduler (Shen et al., 2024)
text:
Critique
No theoretical basis for the power law. The relationship \(\eta_\text{opt} \propto \beta \cdot T^{-0.51}\) is purely empirical. No goodness-of-fit statistics (R², confidence intervals) are reported — validation is visual. The exponent \(\approx -0.5\) hints at a connection to noise-scale or gradient variance arguments, but this is not explored.
Continual learning is claimed but not tested. The paper motivates the schedule partly through continual-learning advantages (no need to prespecify total steps), but presents zero experiments on mid-training resume, continual pretraining, or domain adaptation.
No intermediate checkpoint evaluation. Despite criticizing cosine’s poor intermediate checkpoints, the paper never evaluates “anytime” performance — no plots of downstream accuracy at intermediate points during training.
Unexplained coefficient change. Section 3 derives \(a = 4.6\) empirically; Section 4 deploys \(a = 4\) without justification.
3B baselines are not controlled. The 3B comparisons are against external models (Qwen, Gemma, SmolLM) with different architectures, data mixtures, and training recipes. This makes it impossible to isolate the scheduler’s contribution at the 3B scale.
Missing details. The 3B data mixture is described only as “open-source datasets with permissive licenses.” No compute budget (GPU hours, FLOPs) is reported. No training loss curves comparing schedulers are shown.
muP is load-bearing. The entire framework depends on muP for cross-model-size transfer of the power-law coefficients. Without muP, it is unclear whether the fitted \(a\) and \(b\) would generalize — but the paper does not ablate this.

title: Review: Power Scheduler (Shen et al., 2024)
text:
Our replication
We swept amplitude \(\in [1.0, 5.0]\) (scales peak LR) and power_law_exp \(\in [-0.8, -0.4]\) (\(\alpha\)) over 95 runs at 37M scale on C4, training for 2900 steps with the same model architecture and batch size as our cosine baseline (\(\text{lr}_\text{base} = 0.0073\), cosine_learning_rate_final_fraction = 0.1). Three additional runs at 13M use default power-law hyperparameters.


Code
import json
import os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

pio.renderers.default = "plotly_mimetype+notebook_connected"

EXPERIMENTS_DIR_37M = Path("../clearml_experiments/c4_a100x8x4_37m/power_scheduler")
BASELINE_DIR_37M = Path("../clearml_experiments/c4_a100x8x4_37m/main")
EXPERIMENTS_DIR_13M = Path("../clearml_experiments/c4_a100x8x4_13m/power_scheduler")
BASELINE_DIR_13M = Path("../clearml_experiments/c4_a100x8x4_13m/main")

TRACKED_FIELDS = [
    ("amplitude", "Amplitude"),
    ("power_law_exp", "Power_Law_Exp"),
    ("steps", "Steps"),
    ("learning_rate", "Learning_Rate"),
]

SHARED_DEFAULTS = {
    "steps": 2900,
}


def calculate_ema(data, smoothing=0.97):
    ema = [data[0]]
    for value in data[1:]:
        ema.append(ema[-1] * smoothing + value * (1 - smoothing))
    return ema


def _read_csv(path):
    if not path.exists():
        return None
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    return df["step"].tolist(), df["value"].tolist()


def _parse_config(metadata):
    hp = metadata.get("hyperparameters", {})
    config = {}
    for key, val in hp.items():
        if not key.startswith("Hydra/"):
            continue
        suffix = key.removeprefix("Hydra/")
        if suffix.startswith("model."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("model.")] = val
        elif suffix.startswith("training."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("training.")] = val
        elif suffix.startswith("flat_tokens."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("flat_tokens.")] = val
    return config


def _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="power_scheduler"):
    metadata = json.loads((exp_dir / "metadata.json").read_text())
    if metadata["status"] != "completed":
        return
    task_id = metadata["experiment_id"]
    config_data[task_id] = _parse_config(metadata)
    loss_csv = _read_csv(exp_dir / "metrics" / "loss_loss.csv")
    if loss_csv is None:
        return
    steps, loss = loss_csv
    name = metadata["experiment_name"]
    for prefix in ["c4_a100x8x4_37m_", "c4_a100x8x4_13m_", "c4_sp_37m_", "13m_"]:
        if name.startswith(prefix):
            name = name[len(prefix):]
            break
    metrics_data[task_id] = {
        "name": name,
        "steps": steps,
        "loss": loss,
        "final_loss": calculate_ema(loss)[-1],
        "kind": kind,
    }


def get_metrics_data(experiments_dir, baseline_dir=None):
    metrics_data, config_data = {}, {}
    if experiments_dir.exists():
        for exp_dir in sorted(experiments_dir.iterdir()):
            if exp_dir.is_dir():
                _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="power_scheduler")
    if baseline_dir is not None and baseline_dir.exists():
        for exp_dir in sorted(baseline_dir.iterdir()):
            if exp_dir.is_dir():
                _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="baseline")
    return metrics_data, config_data


def filter_metrics(metrics_data, config_data=None, kinds=None, has_amplitude=None, steps=None):
    out = {}
    for guid, d in metrics_data.items():
        if kinds is not None and d["kind"] not in kinds:
            continue
        cfg = (config_data or {}).get(guid, {})
        if has_amplitude is True and "amplitude" not in cfg:
            continue
        if has_amplitude is False and "amplitude" in cfg:
            continue
        if steps is not None:
            run_steps = cfg.get("steps", SHARED_DEFAULTS.get("steps"))
            if run_steps is not None and int(run_steps) != steps:
                continue
        out[guid] = d
    return out


def get_top_k_experiments(loss_data, k=None, ema_smoothing=0.97):
    sorted_exps = sorted(loss_data.items(), key=lambda x: calculate_ema(x[1]["loss"], smoothing=ema_smoothing)[-1])
    return dict(sorted_exps[:k] if k else sorted_exps)


def plot_loss_data(loss_data, ema_smoothing=0.97, top_k=None, opacity=0.25, title=None):
    if top_k:
        loss_data = get_top_k_experiments(loss_data, k=top_k, ema_smoothing=ema_smoothing)
    palette = ["#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728", "#9467bd", "#8c564b",
               "#e377c2", "#7f7f7f", "#bcbd22", "#17becf"]
    fig = go.Figure()
    for i, (_, data) in enumerate(loss_data.items()):
        steps, loss = data["steps"], data["loss"]
        ema = calculate_ema(loss, smoothing=ema_smoothing)
        color = palette[i % len(palette)]
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=steps, y=loss, mode="lines", name=data["name"],
            legendgroup=data["name"], showlegend=False,
            line=dict(color=color, width=1), opacity=opacity, hoverinfo="skip",
        ))
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=steps, y=ema, mode="lines", name=data["name"],
            legendgroup=data["name"],
            line=dict(color=color, width=2),
            hovertemplate="%{fullData.name}<br>step=%{x}<br>loss=%{y:.4f}<extra></extra>",
        ))
    fig.update_layout(
        title=title,
        xaxis_title="Steps", yaxis_title="Loss",
        height=600, hovermode="closest",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="top", y=-0.15, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
        margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=40, b=80),
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
    fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
    fig.show()


def get_eval_metrics_table(metrics_data, config_data, top_k=None, extra_fields=None):
    rows = []
    for guid, d in metrics_data.items():
        if d["final_loss"] is None:
            continue
        fields = {}
        cfg = config_data.get(guid, {})
        display_fields = TRACKED_FIELDS if extra_fields is None else TRACKED_FIELDS + extra_fields
        for config_key, display_key in display_fields:
            val = cfg.get(config_key)
            if val is None:
                val = SHARED_DEFAULTS.get(config_key)
            if val is not None:
                try:
                    fields[display_key] = float(val)
                except (ValueError, TypeError):
                    fields[display_key] = val
        name = d["name"]
        rows.append({"Name": name if len(name) < 40 else name[:37] + "...", "Final_Loss": float(d["final_loss"]), **fields})
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("Final_Loss").reset_index(drop=True)
    if top_k:
        df = df.head(top_k)
    return df.style.format(precision=6).set_properties(**{"text-align": "left"})

    if subset:
        print(f"{label}: {len(subset)} runs, best final loss = {min(d['final_loss'] for d in subset.values()):.4f}")

title: Review: Power Scheduler (Shen et al., 2024)
text:
Assessment
The paper’s core empirical observation — that optimal LR scales as \(\beta \cdot T^{-0.51}\) — is interesting and the near-\(-0.5\) exponent deserves theoretical follow-up. But the practical claims are undersupported:

The controlled 1B comparison shows a 0.5-point improvement over cosine, well within noise for zero-shot evals. The 3B comparison is uncontrolled (different models, data, training recipes).
The headline advantage — no need to specify total steps — is shared with WSD, which performs comparably in their own Table 5.
Our 95-run sweep at 37M finds that the best power-law configuration matches but does not beat cosine, consistent with the paper’s small margins being within noise at our scale.
The claimed continual-learning and anytime-checkpoint benefits are never empirically validated.

The Power Scheduler is a reasonable alternative to cosine decay, but the evidence does not support adopting it over WSD or cosine in practice. The main contribution is the scaling law observation itself, not the schedule.

title: Review: Power Scheduler (Shen et al., 2024)
text:
Limitations of our replication

All HPO runs train for 2900 steps. The three extended runs (5800, 11600 steps) do not cover the full amplitude/exponent grid.
37M parameters is below the scale where schedule differences typically become decisive.
Runs with amplitude \(\geq 4.5\) hit a learning rate cap at 0.02, making the amplitude parameter ineffective above that threshold.
The 13M power-law runs use default hyperparameters and do not sweep amplitude or exponent.
No eval loss or downstream task measurements beyond training loss are available.

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text: Meta’s Large Concept Models replace next-token prediction with next concept-embedding prediction. The architecture has three stages: a token-to-concept encoder, a concept-level decoder, and a concept-to-token decoder. By operating on concept embeddings instead of tokens, the KV cache for the concept decoder scales as \(L / k\) instead of \(L\), where \(k\) is the concept size in tokens. A jointly-trained variant was tested and found to underperform a standard transformer at all concept sizes, including concept_size=1 where no compression occurs.

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Architecture
The three stages are:

Encoder. Tokens attend to all tokens in the same block and all previous blocks (\(\leq i\)), then a reduction operation collapses each block of \(k\) tokens into a single concept embedding.
Concept decoder. Standard causal transformer over the sequence of concept embeddings.
Token decoder. Causal self-attention over tokens, plus cross-attention to concept embeddings from blocks \(< i\).

All masks are combined with segment masks to prevent attention across packed sequence boundaries. The encoder mask uses strict inequality at the block level: tokens in block \(i\) attend only to tokens in blocks \(< i\). This prevents future leakage, since concept \(i\) is consumed by the token decoder to predict tokens in block \(i\).

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Compression strategies
Four compression strategies were tested for collapsing tokens within a block:








Strategy
Formula
Description




Sum
\(c = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i\)
Element-wise sum of token representations


Max
\(c_j = \max_i\, x_{i,j}\)
Element-wise max over token representations


Learned weighted sum
\(c = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i x_i,\quad w \in \mathbb{R}^k\) learnable
Learnable scalar weight per position within the block


Attention
\(c = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\tfrac{q^\top (XW_K)^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right)\!(XW_V)W_O\)
Constant learned query \(q\) attends over projected block tokens



Implementation: forward_pass, encoder_body, concept_decoder_body, token_decoder_body.

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
What is actually compressed
The token decoder still runs full causal self-attention over all \(L\) tokens. Only the cross-attention KV cache scales as \(L/k\). The theoretical KV compression at concept size \(k\) is:
\[\frac{n_\text{self-attn} \cdot L + n_\text{cross-attn} \cdot L/k}{(n_\text{self-attn} + n_\text{cross-attn}) \cdot L}\]
At concept_size=8 with equal self-attention and cross-attention layers, this is a 56% compression in KV bytes. No memory or latency measurements were taken; the results below are loss-only.

Divergences from the LCM paper







LCM paper
This implementation




Pretrained SONAR encoder/decoder
Jointly trained from scratch


Dynamic sentence-level concepts (10–20 tokens, ≤200 chars)
Fixed-size token blocks


Diffusion-based concept decoder (best variant)
Autoregressive concept decoder only





Code
import json
import os
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

pio.renderers.default = "plotly_mimetype+notebook_connected"

LCM_EXPERIMENTS_DIR = Path("../clearml_experiments/slim_v4_32_31m/lcm")
BASELINE_1024_DIR = Path("../clearml_experiments/slim_v4_32_31m/main/slim_v4_32_31m_lcm_baseline_b5ed44ff59864cf69c4f84d4ad2db5be")

# Window during which the encoder/cross-attention mask used `>=` and leaked within-block
# future tokens. Every block_size>1 run trained in this window beats the baseline; the fix
# (strict `>`) was in place before 2025-01-04 16:16 UTC and after 2025-01-05 06:26 UTC.
LEAKY_WINDOW = (
    datetime(2025, 1, 4, 22, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
    datetime(2025, 1, 5, 6, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
)

TRACKED_FIELDS = [
    ("block_size", "Block_Size"),
    ("layers", "Concept_Decoder_Layers"),
    ("n_e_layers", "Encoder_Layers"),
    ("n_t_layers", "Token_Decoder_Layers"),
    ("reduction_strategy", "Reduction_Strategy"),
    ("n_kv", "Attention_Heads"),
    ("learning_rate", "Learning_Rate"),
    ("d_model", "d_model"),
    ("d_ff", "d_ff"),
]
# `model.layers` means concept decoder depth in LCM rows and total transformer
# depth in baseline rows; route it to a different display column per kind.
DISPLAY_KEY_OVERRIDES = {"baseline": {"layers": "Transformer_Layers"}}

# Defaults for runs that didn't override these fields. The baseline
# (configs/slim_v4-32_31m.yaml) and the LCM runs (configs/slim_v4-8_31m.yaml)
# inherit the same model section from configs/slim_base.yaml.
SHARED_DEFAULTS = {
    "d_model": 256,
    "d_ff": 1024,
    "n_kv": 16,
    "layers": 8,
    "learning_rate": 1.35e-2,
}


def calculate_ema(data, smoothing=0.97):
    ema = [data[0]]
    for value in data[1:]:
        ema.append(ema[-1] * smoothing + value * (1 - smoothing))
    return ema


def _read_csv(path):
    if not path.exists():
        return None
    df = pd.read_csv(path)
    return df["step"].tolist(), df["value"].tolist()


def _parse_config(metadata):
    hp = metadata.get("hyperparameters", {})
    config = {}
    for key, val in hp.items():
        if not key.startswith("Hydra/"):
            continue
        suffix = key.removeprefix("Hydra/")
        if suffix.startswith("model."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("model.")] = val
        elif suffix.startswith("training."):
            config[suffix.removeprefix("training.")] = val
    return config


def _infer_seq_len(metadata):
    name = metadata["experiment_name"]
    if "seq_len=2048" in name or metadata.get("hyperparameters", {}).get("Hydra/training.token.len") == "2048":
        return 2048
    return 1024


def _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="lcm"):
    metadata = json.loads((exp_dir / "metadata.json").read_text())
    if _infer_seq_len(metadata) != 1024:
        return
    created = datetime.fromisoformat(metadata["created"].replace(" ", "T"))
    if kind == "lcm" and LEAKY_WINDOW[0] <= created < LEAKY_WINDOW[1]:
        kind = "leaky"
    task_id = metadata["experiment_id"]
    config_data[task_id] = _parse_config(metadata)
    loss_csv = _read_csv(exp_dir / "metrics" / "loss_loss.csv")
    if loss_csv is None:
        return
    steps, loss = loss_csv
    name = metadata["experiment_name"].replace("model.", "").removeprefix("slim_v4-32_31m_")
    metrics_data[task_id] = {
        "name": name,
        "steps": steps,
        "loss": loss,
        "final_loss": calculate_ema(loss)[-1],
        "total_steps": steps[-1] + 1,
        "seq_len": _infer_seq_len(metadata),
        "kind": kind,
    }


def get_metrics_data(experiments_dir, baseline_dirs=()):
    metrics_data, config_data = {}, {}
    for exp_dir in sorted(experiments_dir.iterdir()):
        if exp_dir.is_dir():
            _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="lcm")
    for exp_dir in baseline_dirs:
        _load_experiment(exp_dir, metrics_data, config_data, kind="baseline")
    return metrics_data, config_data


def filter_by_steps(metrics_data, total_steps):
    return {k: v for k, v in metrics_data.items() if v["total_steps"] == total_steps}


def filter_metrics(metrics_data, seq_len=None, block_size=None, kinds=("lcm", "baseline"), config_data=None):
    out = {}
    for guid, d in metrics_data.items():
        if seq_len is not None and d["seq_len"] != seq_len:
            continue
        if d["kind"] not in kinds:
            continue
        if block_size is not None and d["kind"] == "lcm":
            cfg = (config_data or {}).get(guid, {})
            if str(cfg.get("block_size")) != str(block_size):
                continue
        out[guid] = d
    return out


def get_top_k_experiments(loss_data, k=None, ema_smoothing=0.97):
    sorted_exps = sorted(loss_data.items(), key=lambda x: calculate_ema(x[1]["loss"], smoothing=ema_smoothing)[-1])
    return dict(sorted_exps[:k] if k else sorted_exps)


def plot_loss_data(loss_data, ema_smoothing=0.97, top_k=None, opacity=0.25):
    if top_k:
        loss_data = get_top_k_experiments(loss_data, k=top_k, ema_smoothing=ema_smoothing)
    palette = ["#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728", "#9467bd", "#8c564b",
               "#e377c2", "#7f7f7f", "#bcbd22", "#17becf"]
    fig = go.Figure()
    for i, (_, data) in enumerate(loss_data.items()):
        steps, loss = data["steps"], data["loss"]
        ema = calculate_ema(loss, smoothing=ema_smoothing)
        color = palette[i % len(palette)]
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=steps, y=loss, mode="lines", name=data["name"],
            legendgroup=data["name"], showlegend=False,
            line=dict(color=color, width=1), opacity=opacity, hoverinfo="skip",
        ))
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=steps, y=ema, mode="lines", name=data["name"],
            legendgroup=data["name"],
            line=dict(color=color, width=2),
            hovertemplate="%{fullData.name}<br>step=%{x}<br>loss=%{y:.4f}<extra></extra>",
        ))
    fig.update_layout(
        xaxis_title="Steps", yaxis_title="Loss",
        height=600, hovermode="closest",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="top", y=-0.15, xanchor="center", x=0.5),
        margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=20, b=80),
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
    fig.update_yaxes(showgrid=True, gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)")
    fig.show()


def get_eval_metrics_table(metrics_data, config_data, top_k=None):
    rows = []
    for guid, d in metrics_data.items():
        if d["final_loss"] is None:
            continue
        fields = {}
        cfg = config_data[guid]
        for config_key, display_key in TRACKED_FIELDS:
            val = cfg.get(config_key)
            if val is None:
                val = SHARED_DEFAULTS.get(config_key)
            if val is None:
                continue
            key = DISPLAY_KEY_OVERRIDES.get(d["kind"], {}).get(config_key, display_key)
            fields[key] = float(val) if config_key == "learning_rate" else (val if config_key == "reduction_strategy" else int(val))
        name = d["name"]
        rows.append({"Name": name if len(name) < 25 else name[:25] + "...", "Final Loss": float(d["final_loss"]),
                      "Steps": d["total_steps"], **fields})
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("Final Loss").reset_index(drop=True)
    if top_k:
        df = df.head(top_k)
    return df.style.format(precision=6).set_properties(**{"text-align": "left"})


metrics_data, config_data = get_metrics_data(LCM_EXPERIMENTS_DIR, baseline_dirs=[BASELINE_1024_DIR])

concept_size=1 control

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Limitations
Compute matching across LCM variants is approximate. The 4–8x FLOP range is a rough bound, not a controlled comparison along a single axis (parameters, training FLOPs, or inference FLOPs).
31M parameters at \(L\) = 1024 is below the regime where LCM-style methods are expected to help. The LCM paper’s claims center on long-context semantic compression. A negative result at this scale does not rule out the architecture at larger scales or longer contexts.
No experiment measures actual KV cache memory, prefill latency, or decode latency.

title: Jointly-trained LCM for KV cache compression
text:
Next experiments
The concept_size=1 result narrows the problem to the architecture but does not identify which component is responsible. The following ablations at concept_size=1 would decompose the gap:







Ablation
What it isolates




Skip concept decoder (encoder → token decoder directly)
Cost of the concept decoder


Identity encoder (concept decoder + cross-attention, no learned encoding)
Cost of the encoder


Tied weights across all three sub-networks
Cost of joint optimization


Initialize encoder/decoder from trained baseline
Joint training vs. architecture



Other experiments in rough priority order: disable sequence packing to rule out concept boundary corruption from packed documents; increase context length to \(L\) = 4096 where the LCM thesis applies; measure actual KV cache memory and latency to plot the loss-vs-KV-bytes Pareto frontier; add an auxiliary chunk-reconstruction loss to force concept embeddings to be informative.

title: flat-tokens data format
flat-tokens data format
text:
The flat-tokens data format is a very simple data format for storing language model training data. Unlike some other dataset libraries, it supports efficient seeking after job restarts. It also supports batch size, sequence length, and “sequence packing vs not” being selected at training time.
It is based on the simplest possible design: a concatenation of all tokens in the dataset, together with start indices of each sequence.
